entities.description: Anonymous northern Middle English prose translation and substantial adaptation for female religious; distinct from the metrical version and Caxton abstract.
entities.label: Northern Prose Version of the Rule of St. Benet
notes: The Lansdowne copy is a transcript, not an identified authorial original.

rights.basis: The work predates modern copyright by centuries and this entity excludes modern editorial layers.

entities.description: Anonymous 2,516-line northern Middle English verse translation and adaptation for women; distinct through form, expansion, compression, omission, and chapter order.
entities.label: Northern Metrical Version of the Rule of St. Benet

rights.basis: The work predates modern copyright by centuries and this entity excludes modern editorial layers.

entities.description: Anonymous compendious English adaptation printed as the third part of Caxton's Book of Divers Ghostly Matters for religious men and women with little or no Latin.
entities.label: Caxton Abstract of the Rule of St. Benet
notes: Its explicit requests prayer for the unnamed translator; Caxton is only securely the printer.

rights.basis: The work predates modern copyright by centuries and this entity excludes modern editorial layers.

entities.description: Anonymous procedure for receiving a novice and making her profession, immediately following the prose Rule in Lansdowne MS 378.
entities.label: Northern Lansdowne Ritual for the Ordination of Nuns

rights.basis: The work predates modern copyright by centuries and this entity excludes modern editorial layers.

entities.description: Anonymous Middle English and Latin-formula procedure following the metrical Rule in Cotton MS Vespasian A XXV.
entities.label: Vespasian Ritual for the Ordination of Nuns
notes: Kock calls it an analogous appendage, not a copy or recension of the Lansdowne ritual.

rights.basis: The work predates modern copyright by centuries and this entity excludes modern editorial layers.

entities.description: The abstract Latin monastic Rule attributed to Benedict, common source behind the three distinct Middle English adaptations.
entities.label: Rule of St Benedict

rights.basis: The work predates modern copyright by centuries and this entity excludes modern editorial layers.

next_steps: Compare each with critical Latin recensions and vernacular Rule traditions.
Do not infer one Middle English ancestor from the shared Latin source.
openQuestions.question: Which Latin or intermediary exemplars underlie each Rule adaptation?

next_steps: Collate them with medieval English profession and consecration ordines.
Retain separate-work modeling unless dependence or a recension relationship is demonstrated.
openQuestions.question: Do the two rituals share a recoverable textual ancestor beyond analogous institutional function?

next_steps: Request current Cambridge record and reproduction information.
Preserve physical request routes without asserting permanent non-digitization.
openQuestions.question: Can public facsimiles or authorized reproductions of the two manuscript witnesses and CUL AB.4.64 be obtained?

next_steps: Consult current hand descriptions, provenance work, and specialist bibliography.
Keep Caxton as printer absent positive translator evidence.
openQuestions.question: Can any adapter, translator, ritual author, or manuscript scribe be identified more precisely?

reviewNotes: The three Rule texts are separate Middle English works: prose paraphrase, metrical adaptation, and Caxton abstract. Their shared relationship is to Benedict's Latin Rule, not to one Middle English work.
The two rituals are separate works. Kock calls the Vespasian text analogous; different incipits, extents, wording, and witnesses do not establish a recension relation.
Benedict is source author of the Latin Rule only. Middle English adapters and ritual authors remain anonymous.
Caxton is printer only; the Caxton text names no translator. Kock is editor/transcriber/collator/commentator; Furnivall is limited to running-head summaries.
Manuscript scribes remain unknown and are distinct from work creators. Savile and Anstis are only possible Cotton owners.
Michigan, Google, Internet Archive, and Toronto are digitization/delivery providers, not intellectual creators.
No current public whole-object facsimile was found for the BL witnesses or CUL copy; this is dated negative evidence.
Morgan PML 704 is imperfect and collateral. TCP combines multiple microfilm copies and is not the exact CUL AB.4.64 state.
Public-domain findings for modern editions are U.S.-specific; scan derivatives remain unknown without item-specific licenses.
U-M's current No Copyright-United States assertion and the legacy restrictive XML notice remain separate.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a pinned copy of University of Michigan AHA2736. It encodes Kock's 1902 title page and pp. 1-158: three distinct anonymous Middle English adaptations of Benedict's Latin Rule, two distinct anonymous ordination rituals, and Kock's notes, while omitting the introduction and glossaries. Kock transcribed the prose Rule and Lansdowne ritual from Lansdowne MS 378, the metrical Rule and Vespasian ritual from Cotton MS Vespasian A XXV, and the Caxton abstract from CUL AB.4.64. The metrical text and Vespasian ritual also descend editorially from prior Böddeker and Maskell editions newly collated by Kock; Holthausen and Kölbing are supporting criticism. EEBO-TCP is a collateral composite transcription, not Kock's exact source. Nineteen cached source deliverables preserve the evidence and rights framework.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English adapters, translators, and ritual authors; Benedict of Nursia is the source author for the three Rule texts
catalogSummary.date: Rule and ritual witnesses dated to the first quarter or broader fifteenth century; Caxton abstract printed about 1491; edited selection first published 1902
genres: Ritual for the ordination or profession of nuns
catalogSummary.region: England; the Lansdowne texts are northern, Kock groups the Cotton Rule and ritual in his Northern-text linguistic discussion while noting a more southerly stamp in the metrical Rule, the British Library gives English origin for the Cotton component, and the Caxton abstract was printed at Westminster
catalogSummary.title: Three Middle-English Versions of the Rule of St. Benet and Two Contemporary Rituals for the Ordination of Nuns

catalogingSubject.label: Three Middle-English Versions of the Rule of St. Benet and Two Contemporary Rituals for the Ordination of Nuns
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Ernst A. Kock's 1902 edited selection of five distinct anonymous Middle English texts: a northern prose adaptation of Benedict's Rule, a northern metrical adaptation, the Caxton-press compendious abstract, and two distinct ordination rituals respectively appended to the Lansdowne and Cotton Rule witnesses. The three Rule texts are independent version-level works derived from the same Latin Rule, not three witnesses of one Middle English work. The analogous rituals have different texts and witnesses and are not asserted to be recensions of one work. The encoded unit also contains Kock's title page and notes, but omits his introduction and glossaries.

contentParts.label: Encoded title page
contentParts.summary: Kock's edition title, editorship statement, and 1902 imprint.

contentParts.label: I. Northern Prose Version of the Rule of St. Benet
contentParts.summary: An anonymous extensive prose translation and adaptation for female religious, surviving in Lansdowne MS 378.

contentParts.label: II. Northern Metrical Version of the Rule of St. Benet

locators.value: DIV1 NODE AHA2736:2; PB 48-118; 2,516 L elements

contentParts.summary: An anonymous 2,516-line verse translation and adaptation for women, surviving in Cotton MS Vespasian A XXV.

contentParts.label: III. Caxton Abstract of the Rule of St. Benet
contentParts.summary: An anonymous compendious prose abstract of Benedict's Rule printed as the third part of Caxton's Book of Divers Ghostly Matters, for religious men and women with little or no Latin.

contentParts.label: IV. Northern Lansdowne Ritual for the Ordination of Nuns
contentParts.summary: An anonymous prose procedure for receiving a novice and making her profession, transmitted immediately after the prose Rule in Lansdowne MS 378.

contentParts.label: V. Vespasian Ritual for the Ordination of Nuns
contentParts.summary: An anonymous prose and Latin-formula procedure for making a nun, transmitted after the metrical Rule in Cotton MS Vespasian A XXV and previously printed by Maskell.

contentParts.label: Notes

locators.value: DIV1 NODE AHA2736:6; PB 151-157, continuing through printed p. 158

contentParts.summary: Kock's textual, linguistic, and explanatory notes on the first four edited texts; the section has no separate Vespasian-ritual division.

assertion.notes: This dates the surviving witness and first known attestation, not the lost originals or adaptation acts.
The later heading to the ordination ritual is not used to redetermine the date of its main text.

dateStatements.display: Cotton MS Vespasian A XXV, ff. 66-126: first quarter of the fifteenth century, with later additions

extent.description: Five historical texts in six body DIV1 elements because Kock's notes form the sixth division. The XML has 2,516 L elements in 50 Q1 groups for the metrical Rule, 176 P elements across the five historical texts, 7 P elements in the notes, 283 NOTE1 editorial annotations, 158 PB markers, and 247 manuscript or signature MILESTONE elements. The encoded title page precedes the body. Counts describe the encoding, not an inferred original medieval structure.

formStatements.description: Four prose works, one verse work, and modern prose notes

genres.label: Ritual for the ordination or profession of nuns

scope.description: Chapter lemmata, liturgical formulas, prayers, and rubrics embedded in the Middle English texts

notes: The XML encodes the edition title page and printed pp. 1-158. Kock's introduction (pp. ix-xlix) and glossaries (pp. 159-218) remain outside the U-M/General Corpus text.
Benedict is the author of the abstract Latin Rule source, not the direct author of the three differently adapted Middle English works or the rituals.
Caxton is securely the printer of the approximately 1491 source book. The reviewed sources do not identify him as translator, abstractor, or author of the Rule text.

Witness dates are first-attestation evidence, not silently converted into composition dates. Unknown scribes remain unknown; the Cotton catalog's Henry Savile and John Anstis are possible former owners, not textual creators.

next_steps: Compare each adaptation against modern critical recensions of Benedict's Latin Rule and other vernacular Rule traditions.
Do not infer one shared Middle English ancestor merely from the shared Latin source.
openQuestions.question: Which Latin or intermediary exemplars underlie each of the three Middle English Rule adaptations?

next_steps: Collate the full rituals against medieval English profession and consecration ordines.
Retain separate-work modeling unless textual dependence or a recension relationship is demonstrated.
openQuestions.question: Do the Lansdowne and Vespasian ordination rituals share a recoverable textual ancestor beyond analogous institutional function?

next_steps: Consult manuscript hand descriptions, institutional provenance research, and current specialist bibliography.
Keep Caxton as printer rather than translator or abstractor absent positive evidence.
openQuestions.question: Can any adapter, translator, ritual author, or manuscript scribe be identified more precisely?

assertion.notes: The label is linguistic and editorial, not proof of one composition place or common author.

Kock treats the text in his Northern-text linguistic apparatus but says that its language has a more southerly stamp; the British Library Cotton record gives English origin without a northern-dialect claim.
This is Kock's editorial grouping of the ritual with the Northern texts, not a British Library catalog assertion; the current Cotton record gives English origin only.
Westminster is the place of printing, not a demonstrated composition or translation place.

notes: This source-author role excludes the anonymous Middle English wording and adaptation, both ordination rituals, and Kock's editorial interventions.

scope.description: Authorship of the underlying Latin Rule represented through the three Middle English adaptations

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous northern prose adapter and translator
Anonymous northern prose adapter and translator

assertion.notes: Translation is mediated by free paraphrase and adaptation rather than word-for-word rendering.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous northern metrical adapter and translator
Anonymous northern metrical adapter and translator

assertion.notes: The explicit requests prayer for the translator but does not name Caxton or another individual.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous author of the Lansdowne ordination ritual
Anonymous author of the Vespasian ordination ritual

assertion.notes: For previously printed texts Kock also re-edited and collated earlier transcriptions against the source witnesses.

scope.description: Modern content labels in the running heads on Kock pp. 1-140

summaries.value: Kock's selection juxtaposes three independently adapted Middle English forms of Benedict's Latin Rule: an extensive northern prose paraphrase for female religious, a 2,516-line verse version treated in Kock's Northern-text apparatus but described as having a more southerly stamp, and a short Caxton-press abstract addressed to religious men and women. It then prints two distinct procedures for receiving, professing, or ordaining nuns, one transmitted with the Lansdowne prose Rule and one with the Cotton metrical Rule. Kock's notes explain readings and editorial decisions.

The three Rule texts share Benedict's Latin Rule as an abstract source but differ in prose or verse form, chapter coverage, order, expansion, omission, and witness history. They are modeled as distinct Middle English works. Kock calls the Vespasian ritual an analogous appendage to the Lansdowne ritual; absent evidence of direct copying or a shared textual exemplar, the rituals are also distinct works rather than recensions.

titles.value: Three Middle-English Versions of the Rule of St. Benet and Two Contemporary Rituals for the Ordination of Nuns
Northern Prose Version of the Rule of St. Benet
Northern Metrical Version of the Rule of St. Benet
Caxton Abstract of the Rule of St. Benet
Northern Lansdowne Ritual for the Ordination of Nuns
Vespasian Ritual for the Ordination of Nuns
Son, Herkyn þe commandementis of þe mastir
All þat wyll lely lyue in land
Here felowyth a compendious abstracte translate into englysshe out of the holy rule of saynte Benet for men and wymmen
This is þe furme how A Nouice sall be made & resaiuid to religiun
Here bygyns þe manere forto make a nun
